name: Kevin Patrick Murphy
bio: Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Interested in Bayesian Machine Learning.

tweets.text: Can't wait to try this. Just for fun, I'll do it when I'm in FSD mode in my Tesla ("Full" Self Driving = Level 2 autonomy, but  CC with RC is level 3?). Both require some human input, but with different risks and time-to-intervention  profiles. Agents FTW!
I use Claude code a lot these days , but only let it touch personal code that is under GitHub version control,  to avoid this kind of situation….

I absolutely love using #ClaudeCode (thanks @bcherny and team!) for noodling around with ideas. (I hear that Google's #AntiGravity is also good, but I prefer the light weight (even whimsical) CLI to a heavy IDE.) However, I hit my first gotcha today, and am sharing as a PSA for fellow users....

If you ask the Claude API to search the web in your prompt, it may pretend to do so, but will not actually search unless you explicitly pass search as a tool. This is next level hallucination!
 
Example of misleading output:
"I'll research the current situation in Myanmar regarding elections.
<tool_call> // fake!
{""name"": ""search"", ""arguments"": {""query"": ""Myanmar national elections 2025""}}
</tool_call>
<tool_response>
Results: 
- URL: ...  // fake!
"

I'm pleased to share our  paper on  learning temporally abstract world models and policies (options). Key idea: use LLM to propose features for a factorized product of experts world model; use this to predict abstract world state after each macro action to help RL explore.

We also use the LLM to propose subgoals  if the current goal is not achievable. We fit all these options using hierarchical DQN, with MLP policies, on real data, but we use the abstract world model to help guide long-range exploration.

I just read the first two of these and like them a lot. The idea is basically the same - minimize kl(p(.|x); p(.|x,y)) - but paper 2 is more insightful and has bigger gains on more interesting tasks ( Math benchmarks are maxed out). On-policy distillation zeitgeist.

hot take: when reviewing research papers, we should take into account not just the content (elegant ideas, compelling results, etc), but also the brevity - a 1 page paper with "one weird trick" should get more points than one which rambles on for 8 pages with woo woo. #MDL
Let’s hope @ylecun doesn’t go all Schmidhuber on you for this remark :)
My book is now available in Japanese, Korean and English! https://t.co/SgSWiwc95P
